identity.title: All Other Converted Paper Product Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly provide custom paper or paperboard converting, die-cut components, molded-pulp trays, egg cartons, food containers, protective packaging, and related specification-driven products to external manufacturers, food producers, distributors, brands, or institutions.
lens.excluded_activities: Paper novelties and decorative products sold mainly through transactional consumer channels; captive converting; paperboard boxes, bags, coated paper, stationery, and sanitary products classified elsewhere; pure distribution; shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring purchase orders, supply programs, contract converting, customer-owned-material processing, custom tooling programs, and specification-driven per-unit product sales, often with inventory release schedules, quality requirements, and repeat production runs.
lens.deviation_from_default: The residual code mixes molded-fiber packaging, industrial contract converting, die-cut components, crepe paper, and paper novelties. The lens keeps repeat outsourced custom converting and molded-fiber supply while excluding primarily transactional novelty sales because one service-transfer screen across those models would be incoherent.

a.bridge: BLS shows paper-goods machine operators are a major occupation in the broader converted-paper industry, and O*NET describes physical setup, machine control, inspection, and adjustment. A cross-economy GPT study supplies only an upper-context benchmark for information-task exposure. I map low exposure to physical molding, converting, tooling, maintenance, packing, and handling, and higher exposure to estimating, CAD/specification support, scheduling, purchasing, quality records, service, and administration. The national GPT benchmark likely overstates this production-heavy industry's exposure.
a.rationale: Custom work creates more information-rich quoting, engineering, setup planning, scheduling, and quality documentation than commodity continuous production, while most direct conversion and molded-fiber labor remains physical. AI can reduce time in RFQ parsing, job costing, design iteration, work instructions, inspection triage, inventory planning, and customer service. Existing mechanical automation is excluded.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS occupation-and-wage extract plus task-level time studies at at least ten custom die-cut and molded-fiber plants, explicitly inventorying current CAD/CAM, machine controls, ERP, vision, and administrative automation.
caveats: No public measure gives wage-weighted not-yet-automated AI exposure for NAICS 322299.
Four-digit occupation data blend several converted-paper industries.
Labor mix varies sharply between molded pulp, paper novelties, and precision contract converting.

rho.bridge: Census industrial-AI research reports 22.8% of US manufacturing plants used any AI in 2021, and Federal Reserve monitoring reports about 18% of firms had adopted AI by year-end 2025. These measure broad current adoption, not five-year implementation of exposed opportunity. I project higher diffusion for packaged quoting, planning, document, vision, and design tools, then discount for small-firm data fragmentation, custom-job variability, quality validation, and legacy integration. Broad adoption can overstate meaningful workflow depth.
rho.rationale: Custom specifications and recurring jobs generate structured documents and historical data that support implementation. However, varied substrates, low-volume runs, tooling dependencies, manual exception handling, and limited technical staff constrain rollout. The base assumes practical deployment across several support workflows, not autonomous physical production.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of 322299 firms covering AI use-case deployment, pilot-to-production rates, ERP/CAD/MES integration, exception frequency, customer validation, labor-hour impact, and payback by process type and plant size.
caveats: Industrial AI includes non-generative methods and does not imply labor substitution.
The adoption sources cover all manufacturing or all firms, not the selected LMM lens.
Implementation should be easier in repeat precision-converting programs than in novelty or one-off work.

e.rationale: Contract converting and custom molded-fiber supply are explicit recurring outsourced models: customers specify dimensions, materials, packaging, and release schedules while the converter owns process execution and quality. The residual code also contains novelty, commodity, proprietary-product, and captive operations, so not every LMM firm qualifies.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 112 dataset-estimated LMM firms using Capital IQ, PitchBook, company sites, customer references, and management interviews to classify revenue from recurring external contract conversion versus proprietary or transactional products.
caveats: No official dataset reports recurring contract-converting revenue share.
The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged and may misstate the target-band population.
NAICS classification boundaries are difficult for multi-material die cutters and packaging firms.

s5.bridge: Census reports more than half of US business owners were age 55 or older, while BizBuySell reports completed manufacturing acquisitions rose 16% year over year in Q1 2026. These establish broad succession pressure and transaction activity but not a five-year transfer probability for eligible 322299 firms. I translate them into a bounded five-year rate, discounting closures, internal transitions, asset-only sales, owner dependence, and unsuccessful processes. Sale-platform data likely overstate transferability because it observes selected listings and completed deals.
s5.rationale: The estimated firm pool is relatively broad, and recurring OEM or food-program revenue plus equipment and tooling can support transferability. Aging owners add supply, while customer concentration, specialized know-how, environmental exposure, capex needs, and mixed classification can prevent completed control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year deal logs from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, and packaging-industry brokers, filtered to custom paper converters and molded-fiber firms and reconciled to the eligible denominator.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is not industry- or size-specific.
BizBuySell skews toward smaller Main Street transactions.
Asset sales and internal successions are not necessarily qualifying control transfers.

q.rationale: Per-unit and conversion pricing typically fixes economics between bids or program renewals, while custom tooling, qualified specifications, inventory programs, and reliability raise switching costs. Savings can still pass through through competitive rebids, open-book arrangements, index clauses, customer cost-down demands, and price competition. Faster design, service, and fewer errors should retain better than visible direct-labor savings.
q.change_evidence: Review at least 30 customer contracts and bid histories for open-book costing, substrate pass-through, annual cost-downs, tooling ownership, qualification periods, renewal cadence, and gross-margin changes after automation deployments.
caveats: There is no public evidence measuring AI-benefit pass-through in this residual industry.
Customer and end-market concentration can overwhelm nominal switching costs.
Material and freight volatility can mask the retained productivity benefit.

d5.bridge: USDA projects per-capita table-egg availability increasing from 21.5 dozen in 2025 to 22.9 dozen in 2026, and reports April 2026 egg production up 5% year over year; EPA reports 14.5 million tons of plastic containers and packaging generated in 2018 and identifies molded pulp egg cartons and fruit trays as high-recycled-content products. These are demand drivers and substitution pools, not a direct five-year measure of the full 322299 basket. I infer modest real growth for food and protective molded fiber, offset by mature or declining novelty and miscellaneous paper products. The selected packaging proxies may overstate growth for the heterogeneous code.
d5.rationale: Egg, food, industrial protection, and sustainability-driven fiber packaging support demand, while custom converting is tied to ongoing manufacturing output. Plastic substitution provides upside for molded fiber. Paper novelties, decorative products, and other residual uses may be cyclical or digitally displaced, so the range crosses flat demand despite a modest-growth base.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-volume Census AIES/Economic Census shipments by 322299 product class, customer forecasts from food, egg, electronics, and industrial accounts, import data, and third-party scanner or packaging-substrate data separating molded fiber from novelty and other converting.
caveats: Egg demand captures only one molded-pulp use and can be distorted by flock disease cycles.
Plastic packaging tonnage is a substitution opportunity, not evidence that paper wins the share.
The broad product mix makes one industry-level demand ratio especially uncertain.

o.rationale: Surviving demand for die-cut parts, molded-fiber trays, egg cartons, protective packaging, and other converted products requires physical material transformation, tooling, quality control, and delivery by an accountable operator. Software can automate design, procurement, and ordering, while customers may vertically integrate, import directly, or shift to standardized self-service products; these pathways remove a minority of lens-operator demand.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and win/loss data quantifying conversion to in-house production, direct imports, standardized catalog or digital-fabrication alternatives, and material substitution that removes the independent converter rather than changing suppliers.
caveats: This is conditional on year-five demand and excludes demand decline already captured in d5.
Some custom paper components can migrate to plastic, foam, reusable packaging, or additive manufacturing.
Foreign manufacturers may remain accountable operators but fall outside the US lens.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is recurring custom converting and molded-fiber supply, not the entire miscellaneous-paper residual. Custom specifications and repeat programs create information-rich workflows around a still-physical production core, while sustainable packaging and food uses offer demand support.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate RFQ and specification parsing, estimating, job costing, CAD iteration, work instructions, scheduling, procurement, quality-document review, inspection triage, inventory planning, and customer service. Molding, die cutting, setup, maintenance, packing, and material movement remain equipment- and labor-bound.
narrative.value_capture: Custom tooling, qualification, and release schedules create switching friction and protect some savings between rebids. Capture weakens with open-book contracts, annual cost-downs, material pass-through, and concentrated OEM or food customers.
narrative.firm_availability: A sizable LMM estimate and visible contract-converting models support a potential pipeline. Actual eligibility must be established firm by firm because the code also contains novelty makers, proprietary products, captive plants, and multi-material firms whose classification may be unstable.
narrative.demand_durability: Molded-fiber egg, food, and protective packaging benefits from recurring consumption and potential plastic substitution. Miscellaneous novelty and decorative products are more cyclical and exposed to shifting tastes, producing a wider demand range than packaging alone would suggest.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The residual NAICS definition is the principal research problem. Product mix, customer concentration, tooling ownership, substrate costs, plant capex, environmental obligations, import competition, and classification drift can dominate the outcome. A target dependent on one customer's custom part, without owned tooling or diversified programs, could be a poor acquisition.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat specification-driven converting programs combine customer stickiness with automatable quoting, planning, and quality workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: The residual code's extreme product heterogeneity makes industry-level evidence weak and firm selection decisive.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 322299 All Other Converted Paper Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines the residual industry and lists crepe paper, non-office die-cut paper, paper novelties, and molded-pulp egg cartons, food containers, and trays.

sources.title: Converted Paper Product Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Estimates
sources.supports: Shows paper-goods machine setters/operators/tenders at 63,660 workers and 23.32% of employment in broader converted-paper manufacturing.

sources.title: O*NET: Paper Goods Machine Setters, Operators, and Tenders
sources.supports: Documents core physical tasks such as controlling machines, inspecting finished products, and correcting production problems.

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of Large Language Models
sources.supports: Provides broad US workforce exposure context: about 80% with at least 10% of tasks affected and 19% with at least 50%; not industry-specific.

sources.title: The Adoption of Industrial AI in America
sources.supports: Reports 22.8% of approximately 28,500 US manufacturing establishments used any industrial AI in 2021.

sources.title: Monitoring AI Adoption in the US Economy
sources.supports: Reports about 18% of firms had adopted AI by year-end 2025 based on Census business survey data.

sources.title: Paper Dimensions Custom Paper Converting
sources.supports: Describes recurring contract converting around customer specifications, customer-supplied material, custom packaging, held inventory, and scheduled releases.

sources.title: Advanced Paper Forming Custom Molded Fiber Packaging
sources.supports: Documents US custom molded-pulp design, tooling, and production for industrial, electronics, glass, and other protective packaging uses.

sources.title: Sun Process Converting - Precision Contract Converting
sources.supports: Describes repeat outsourced slitting, sheeting, die cutting, laminating, and packaging for OEMs, manufacturers, and industrial suppliers.

sources.title: Annual Business Survey Visualizations
sources.supports: Census states more than half of US business owners were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report - Market Trends
sources.supports: Reports Q1 2026 manufacturing acquisitions increased 16% year over year and 22% quarter over quarter.

sources.title: Per Capita Availability of Red Meat and Poultry Projected Higher in 2025 and 2026
sources.supports: Projects US per-capita table-egg availability rising from 21.5 dozen in 2025 to 22.9 dozen in 2026, supporting egg-carton demand context.

sources.title: USDA Chickens and Eggs, May 2026
sources.supports: Reports April 2026 US egg production of 8.92 billion, up 5% year over year, including 7.65 billion table eggs.

sources.title: EPA Containers and Packaging: Product-Specific Data
sources.supports: Reports 14.5 million tons of plastic containers and packaging generated in 2018 and identifies trays and egg cartons among plastic packaging forms, grounding a potential substitution pool.
